title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Tom Bailey, who repairs bicycles.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Tom Bailey, who repairs old bicycles and teaches people how to look after them. Tom, when did you first start repairing bikes? || Guest: I learnt because I had to. When I was thirteen, my bike broke just before a race at school. My dad was away, and the local shop was closed. [20]I found a video online, fixed the chain myself, and felt much prouder than I expected[/20]. After that, I wanted to understand every part of the bike. || Interviewer: Did you train as a mechanic? || Guest: I did a short course after college, but most of my learning came from practice. I used to buy broken bikes cheaply and repair them in our garage. [21]The course was useful because it taught me how to work safely and check my repairs properly[/21]. Before that, I sometimes rushed and missed small problems. || Interviewer: You also run workshops. Who usually comes to them? || Guest: A real mix. Some people are serious cyclists, but many just ride to work or to the shops. [22]I'm always pleased when beginners come because they often think bike repair is too difficult for them[/22]. After one morning, they can usually fix a puncture and adjust their brakes. That gives them confidence. || Interviewer: What mistake do bike owners often make? || Guest: They wait until something stops working. A bicycle doesn't need much, but it needs regular care. [23]The most common problem is that people forget to clean and oil the chain[/23]. Then the bike feels slow, and other parts wear out more quickly. Five minutes every week can make a big difference. || Interviewer: Has the number of people cycling changed in your town? || Guest: Yes, definitely. More people started during the pandemic, and many have continued. The new cycle paths help, but they aren't perfect. [24]What has made the biggest difference is that several offices now provide safe places to leave bikes[/24]. People don't want to lock an expensive bike in the street all day. || Interviewer: What are your plans for the business? || Guest: I could open a bigger workshop, but I'm not sure I want that. I like knowing my customers. Next spring, [25]I'm going to start evening classes for teenagers who want to learn practical repair skills[/25]. Some may become mechanics, but the main aim is to make them more independent. || Interviewer: Are modern bikes more difficult to repair than older ones? || Guest: Some are, especially bikes with electric parts, but the basic ideas are the same. I tell people not to be frightened by new technology. They should learn simple jobs first, like checking tyre pressure and making sure the brakes feel right. Those small checks prevent many problems. || Interviewer: Do you think cycling will keep growing in your town? || Guest: I hope so, but it depends on safety. People need routes where they do not feel they are fighting with cars all the time. Families also need clear information about where beginners can ride. If the town continues improving those things, more people will choose a bike for short journeys instead of taking the car. || Interviewer: What should someone carry on a normal bike ride? || Guest: A spare inner tube, a small pump and lights if they may come home late. They do not need a huge bag of tools for every short journey. The main thing is knowing how to use the few things they bring, because tools are only helpful if you have practised.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Tom Bailey, who repairs old bicycles and teaches people how to look after them. Tom, when did you first start repairing bikes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I learnt because I had to. When I was thirteen, my bike broke just before a race at school. My dad was away, and the local shop was closed. I found a video online, fixed the chain myself, and felt much prouder than I expected. After that, I wanted to understand every part of the bike.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Did you train as a mechanic?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I did a short course after college, but most of my learning came from practice. I used to buy broken bikes cheaply and repair them in our garage. The course was useful because it taught me how to work safely and check my repairs properly. Before that, I sometimes rushed and missed small problems.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You also run workshops. Who usually comes to them?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A real mix. Some people are serious cyclists, but many just ride to work or to the shops. I'm always pleased when beginners come because they often think bike repair is too difficult for them. After one morning, they can usually fix a puncture and adjust their brakes. That gives them confidence.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What mistake do bike owners often make?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They wait until something stops working. A bicycle doesn't need much, but it needs regular care. The most common problem is that people forget to clean and oil the chain. Then the bike feels slow, and other parts wear out more quickly. Five minutes every week can make a big difference.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the number of people cycling changed in your town?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, definitely. More people started during the pandemic, and many have continued. The new cycle paths help, but they aren't perfect. What has made the biggest difference is that several offices now provide safe places to leave bikes. People don't want to lock an expensive bike in the street all day.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans for the business?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I could open a bigger workshop, but I'm not sure I want that. I like knowing my customers. Next spring, I'm going to start evening classes for teenagers who want to learn practical repair skills. Some may become mechanics, but the main aim is to make them more independent.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Are modern bikes more difficult to repair than older ones?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some are, especially bikes with electric parts, but the basic ideas are the same. I tell people not to be frightened by new technology. They should learn simple jobs first, like checking tyre pressure and making sure the brakes feel right. Those small checks prevent many problems.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think cycling will keep growing in your town?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I hope so, but it depends on safety. People need routes where they do not feel they are fighting with cars all the time. Families also need clear information about where beginners can ride. If the town continues improving those things, more people will choose a bike for short journeys instead of taking the car.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What should someone carry on a normal bike ride?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A spare inner tube, a small pump and lights if they may come home late. They do not need a huge bag of tools for every short journey. The main thing is knowing how to use the few things they bring, because tools are only helpful if you have practised.

questions.question: Why did Tom first repair a bicycle himself?

options.A: He wanted to win a school prize.
options.B: He had no one else available to help him.
options.C: He was copying something his father did.

questions.explanation: Tom says his dad was away, the shop was closed, and he had to fix the chain himself.

questions.question: What did Tom mainly learn from the course?

options.A: How to make his repairs safe.

questions.explanation: Tom says the course taught him to work safely and check repairs properly.

questions.question: Who does Tom especially like teaching?
questions.explanation: Tom says he is always pleased when beginners come to his workshops.

questions.question: What does Tom say many bike owners forget to do?
questions.explanation: Tom says the most common problem is forgetting to clean and oil the chain.

questions.question: What has encouraged more people to cycle to work?

options.A: Better cycle paths near their homes.

questions.explanation: Tom says the biggest difference is that offices now provide safe places to leave bikes.

questions.question: What does Tom plan to do next spring?
questions.explanation: Tom says he is going to start evening classes for teenagers who want repair skills.
